coredata.dc:title: MXene quantum dots decorated Ni nanoflowers for efficient Cr (VI) reduction
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Yali
Cheng, Yonghua
Li, Xingchuan
Li, Qingqing
Li, Xiaotian
Chu, Ke
coredata.dc:description:
Nickel@MXene quantum dots (Ni@MQDs), as novel flower-like hybrid materials, were firstly prepared through a simple reduction method. The Ni@MQDs exhibited an outstanding catalytic performance for Cr (VI) reduction with a low activation energy (E
a = 18.9 kJ mol−1) and a high kinetic constant (k = 0.4779 min−1) in the presence of formic acid (HCOOH). Density functional theory calculations demonstrated that Ni@MQDs exhibited an upshift of d-band center of active Ni atoms to promote the adsorption of both HCOOH and active H atoms, as well as an improved conductivity to boost the catalytic reaction kinetics, leading to the most favorable catalytic performance. This work may open up a new avenue towards the design and synthesis of novel MQDs-based hybrid catalysts for wastewater treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MXene quantum dots
Ni nanoflowers
Catalyst
Cr(VI) Reduction
Wastewater treatment

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a, b) TEM images of MQDs at different magnifications (inset: HRTEM image). (c) Size distribution of MQDs. (d, e) TEM images of Ni@MQDs at different magnifications. (f) HRTEM image of Ni@MQDs (insets A and B show MQDs layers and Ni (111) planes, respectively). (g) Element mapping images of Ni@MQDs.
(a) XRD patterns of Ni@MQDs and MQDs. (b) XPS survey spectrum of the Ni@MQDs. (c-e) HRXPS spectra of C, Ti and Ni of Ni@MQDs. (f) FTIR spectra of MQDs and Ni@MQDs.
(a) UV–vis absorbance of Ni@MQDs and MQDs. (b) PL spectra of MQDs at different excitation wavelengths. (c) PL spectra of MQDs and Ni@MQDs at an excitation wavelength of 330 nm.

(a) Catalytic activity Ni@MQDs for reduction of Cr (VI) by UV–vis spectra (inset: time-course photograph of the solution). (b, c) Relationships between ln (C t/C 0) and reaction time over (b) different catalysts with HCOOH and (c) different temperatures. Inset (c): linear fitting of lnk versus 1/T. (d) Relationship between ln (C t/C 0) and reaction time over Ni@MQDs using H2 or CO as the reductant. (e) Cycle performance of Ni@MQDs in the catalytic reduction of Cr (VI) with HCOOH. (f) Conversion efficiency of Cr (VI) reduction at different cycles.
(a) TEM image of Ni@MQDs after reduction of Cr (VI). (b) XRD patterns of Ni@MQDs before and after reduction of Cr (VI).

(a–c) Optimized structures and calculated adsorption energies and Mulliken charges of HCOOH adsorption on (a) Ni, (b) Ti3C2-MQDs and (c) Ni@MQDs. (d–f) Optimized structures, adsorption energy and Mulliken charges of H adsorption on (d) Ni, (e) MQDs and (f) Ni@MQDs.

(a) Projected density of states (PDOS) for Ni and heterojunction Ni@MQDs structures. Purple dash lines represent d-band center (εd). (b) Differential charge density maps of Ni@MQDs in the contact interface. Yellow and cyan regions represent the election accumulation and depletion, respectively. (c, d) PDOS of Ni and Ni@MQDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic of the synthesis procedure of Ni@MQD.
Schematic illustration of catalytic mechanism for the Cr (VI) reduction by FA over Ni@MQDs.
Comparison of various catalysts with the present work for the reduction of Cr (VI).

head.ce:title: MXene quantum dots decorated Ni nanoflowers for efficient Cr (VI) reduction

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Lanzhou Jiaotong University, Lanzhou 730070, Gansu, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Lanzhou Jiaotong University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Lanzhou Jiaotong University, Lanzhou 730070, Gansu, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Nickel@MXene quantum dots (Ni@MQDs), as novel flower-like hybrid materials, were firstly prepared through a simple reduction method. The Ni@MQDs exhibited an outstanding catalytic performance for Cr (VI) reduction with a low activation energy (E
a = 18.9 kJ mol−1) and a high kinetic constant (k = 0.4779 min−1) in the presence of formic acid (HCOOH). Density functional theory calculations demonstrated that Ni@MQDs exhibited an upshift of d-band center of active Ni atoms to promote the adsorption of both HCOOH and active H atoms, as well as an improved conductivity to boost the catalytic reaction kinetics, leading to the most favorable catalytic performance. This work may open up a new avenue towards the design and synthesis of novel MQDs-based hybrid catalysts for wastewater treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: Chromium (Cr) is widely used in the chemical, pharmaceutical and textile industries (Pellerin and Booker, 2000; Ellis et al., 2002; Saha et al., 2011; Farooqi et al., 2021). Cr generally exists in two oxidation states of Cr (VI) and Cr (III). The toxicity of Cr (VI) is about one hundred times that of Cr (III), which has been proved to be harmful to humans beings and the environment (Stearns et al., 1995; Elliott and Zhang, 2001). However, Cr (III) is nontoxic, relatively stable, and is an essential nutrient for the human body (Giannakas et al., 2013; Josue et al., 2020). Therefore, it is highly expected to explore an effective and economical treatment process to reduce Cr (VI) in wastewater. Nowadays, the technologies for reducing Cr (VI) to Cr (III) include photocatalysis (Sharma et al., 2019a, 2019b; Kumar et al., 2021), membrane separation (Hafez et al., 2002), biological process (Zakaria et al., 2007), precipitation (Almeida and Boaventura, 1998), and ion exchange (Kocaoba and Akcin, 2002). However, most of these approaches generally suffer from low efficiency, high cost and large chemical dosage. The chemical reduction method has recently gained a noticeable popularity in removing Cr (VI) due to its low cost and high efficiency (Dandapat et al., 2011). As a green chemical, HCOOH has a strong reducing ability and is a promising reductant for Cr (VI) reduction (Park et al., 2017; Islam et al., 2019). Nevertheless, there are still large kinetic obstacles in the reduction of Cr (VI) by HCOOH, which requires effective catalysts to promote the reaction kinetics.

Pd-based catalysts have been widely used for Cr (VI) reduction, but their large-scale commercial applications are plagued by the reserve scarcity, high cost and unsatisfactory stability. Alternatively, earth-abundant Ni-based catalysts have recently attracted considerable interest for catalytic Cr (VI) reduction due to their tunable morphology, favorable catalytic properties, high stability and ease of synthesis (Bhowmik et al., 2014; Park et al., 2019). Nevertheless, most reported Ni catalysts are shaped in nanoparticles (NPs) which are usually unstable and have a strong tendency to aggregate (Carenco et al., 2010; Ali et al., 2020), leading to a decrease in catalytic activity. A feasible way to address this issue is to decorate Ni NPs on various support materials, such as graphene (Bhowmik et al., 2014; Julkapli and Bagheri, 2015), polydopamine (Bakirci et al., 2017), metal-organic frameworks (MOF) (Meek et al., 2011) and carbon dots (de Medeiros et al., 2019), thereby avoiding NP agglomeration and boosting the catalytic activity. As a new generation of two-dimensional (2D) materials, MXenes have attracted a substantial attention due to their excellent conductivity, large surface area and plentiful surface hydrophilic groups (-OH, -O, or -F) (Xue et al., 2017; Shuck et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021). Impressively, MXene quantum dots (MQDs), a new class of QD materials derived from layered MXenes (Xue et al., 2017), have emerged as promising electrode materials for diverse applications of bioimaging, catalysis, sensing, and energy storage (Shuck et al., 2020; Manikandan et al., 2019; Neupane et al., 2021). MQDs not only inherit the intrinsic properties of MXenes, but also exhibit intriguing physicochemical properties owing to quantum confinement effects, rending MQDs promising support material for decorating metal NPs (Rafieerad et al., 2020, 2019). However, the investigations on the synthesis of metal NPs@MQDs and their catalytic activities have been rarely reported.

In this work, we prepared novel flower-like Ni@MQD hybrids through a simple reduction process and examined Ni@MQDs as a catalyst to reduce Cr (VI) to Cr (III). The structures and catalytic properties of the Ni@MQDs were systematically studied. Furthermore, the catalytic Cr (VI) reduction mechanism over Ni@MQDs was profoundly investigated by density functional theory (DFT) calculations.

MQDs were prepared according to the previous report (Xue et al., 2017), and the details are described in Section 1.4 of the Supporting information. The Ni@MQD hybrids were prepared through the chemical reduction method. Briefly, an amount of Nickel (Ⅱ) nitrate was dissolved directly in ethylene glycol. Then, 10 mL Ni (NO3)2·6H2O solution (45 mM) was mixed with 10 mL MQDs aqueous suspension in a 50 mL round bottom flask, followed by adding 1.125 g of hydrazine hydrate into the solution under magnetic stirring for 10 min to obtain a purple solution. Subsequently, NaBH4 (5 mg mL−1) was added dropwise to initiate the reaction. The color of the solution gradually changed to black. After that, the mixture was kept stirring at 60 °C for 1 h. The resulting product was washed with distilled water and ethanol several times through centrifugation and then dried at 60 °C for 6 h in a vacuum oven. The bare Ni NPs were prepared under the same conditions without the addition of MQDs.

The catalytic activity of the Ni@MQDs was evaluated by the reduction of Cr (VI) to Cr (III) with HCOOH. The catalytic process was recorded with a UV–vis spectrophotometer at room temperature. Typically, 0.5 mL of Ni@MQDs suspension (0.38 mg mL−1) was injected into the solution containing 9.5 mL of K2Cr2O7 (1 mM), 9 mL of H2O and 1.5 mL of formic acid (88%). The variation of Cr (VI) concentration with reaction time was monitored at 350 nm. To study the reusability of the catalyst, the catalyst was recovered by high-speed centrifugation, washed with water, and then redispersed in water for the next cycle. For comparison, the control experiments were also conducted under the same condition.

Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were carried out on a Cambridge sequential total energy package (CASTEP). The exchange-correlation potential was described by the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) within the framework of the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) functional (Gu et al., 2020; Chu et al., 2019b). DFT-D method was employed to calculate the van der Waals (vdW) interaction. Electronic energies were computed with the tolerance of 2 × 10−5 eV and a total force of 0.05 eV/Å. The Brillouin zone was sampled with 3 × 3 × 1 k-points, and kinetic cutoff energy of 380 eV was adopted. The Ni@MQDs slab was modeled by 3 × 3 supercell (a = 9.18 Å, a = 9.20 Å, c = 17.20 Å). A vacuum space of 17.20 Å was inserted in y direction to avoid interactions between periodic images.

The ΔE for a given adsorbed species can be expressed as (Chu et al., 2020a, 2020b; Wang et al., 2020)
(1)
E
=
E
ads / s u b s t r a t e
−
E
ads
−
E
s u b s t r a t e
where E ads/substrate, E ads, and E substrate are the total energies for adsorbed species on the substrate, adsorbed species, and bare substrate, respectively.
More detailed information of Materials and Characterization were provided in the Supporting Information (SI).

The TEM images of MQDs shown in Figs. 1a–b and S1 indicate that the prepared MQDs are spherical and well dispersed on a carbon membrane. The HRTEM image of MQDs (Fig. 1b, inset) displays clear lattice fringes with an interplane spacing of 0.193 nm, which can be indexed to (101) plane of Ti3C2 (Neupane et al., 2021). The average size of MQDs is estimated to be 5.96 nm (Fig. 1c). Fig. 1d, e show the TEM images of Ni@MQDs at different magnifications. Visibly, Ni nanoflowers are uniformly dispersed and wrapped by MQD nanolayers. The average size of Ni@MQDs is calculated to be 35 nm (Fig. S2). The HRTEM image of Ni@MQDs(Fig. 1f) reveals two lattice distances of 0.193 and 0.209 nm, corresponding to (101) and (111) planes of MQDs and Ni, respectively (Fig. 1f) (Rafieerad et al., 2020; Diánez et al., 2016). As depicted in the elemental mapping images (Fig. 1g), in addition to C/O/Ti, Ni element is uniformly distributed in the whole composite structure. The formation mechanism of Ni@MQDs is proposed as follows ( Scheme 1). When nickel salt is mixed with MQDs suspension, Ni2+ ions are attached to the surface of MQDs through electrostatic interaction. After adding NaBH4 as a reducing agent, Ni2+ ions can be in-situ reduced to Ni atoms as seeds for nucleation of small-sized Ni NPs. The strong magnetic interactions among Ni NPs enable the generation of flower-like Ni NPs with simultaneous formation of MQD nanolayers on the surface of Ni NPs (Guo et al., 2015), obtaining Ni@MQDs.

Fig. 2a shows the XRD patterns of MQDs and Ni@MQDs. It is seen that MQDs display two major diffraction peaks at 18° and 29°, corresponding to (004) and (008) planes of Ti3C2, respectively (Chandra et al., 2014). As for Ni@MQDs, one diffraction peak of MQDs is disappeared, instead, two insignificant diffraction peaks are emerged, which are ascribed to (111) and (200) planes of the face centered cubic structured (fcc) Ni (JCPDS No. 04-0850) (Chandra et al., 2014; Yakymovych and Ipser, 2017). The (111) plane of Ni shows the most significant diffraction peak, indicating that Ni NPs grow along the (111) plane, which accords with the HRTEM observation (Fig. 1f).

Fig. 2b shows the XPS survey spectrum of Ni@MQDs, indicating the coexistence of C, Ti, O, and Ni elements. The high-resolution XPS (HRXPS, Fig. 2c) spectrum of C 1s shows four peaks centered at 283.8, 284.8, 285.5, and 288.7 eV, corresponding to C-Ti, C-C, C-O and CO groups of MQDs, respectively (Xue et al., 2017). In addition, the HRXPS spectrum of Ti 2p shows two deconvoluted peaks (Fig. 2d), assigned to Ti 2p1/2 and Ti 2p3/2, respectively. Meanwhile, the Ni 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 2e) displays two prominent peaks of 2p1/2 (858.2 eV) and 2p3/2 (879.4 eV) for Ni2+. The other two small peaks centered at 853.1 and 873.9 eV are assigned to 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 for Ni0, respectively (Ungureanu et al., 2013). This finding indicates that Ni NPs in Ni@MQDs are mostly hydroxylated. The same phenomenon has also been previously reported for other nano-sized Ni materials (Ungureanu et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2019).

Additionally, the functional groups on MQDs and Ni@MQDs are investigated by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), as shown in Fig. 2f. For MQDs, there are obvious absorption bands at 3410, 1604, 784 and 560 cm−1, corresponding to -OH, CO, Ti-O and Ti-C stretching vibrations, respectively (Rafieerad et al., 2020), indicating that the prepared MQDs are terminated with multifunctional groups, making MQDs easier to coordinate with Ni2+. The FTIR spectrum of Ni@MQDs is similar to that of MQDs, but some of the absorption peaks become weaker, which can be attributed to the interactions between the functional groups on MQDs and Ni (Rafieerad et al., 2020; Guo et al., 2015).

To investigate the optical properties of MQDs and Ni@MQDs, the UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra are collected in Fig. 3a–c. As shown in Fig. 3a, the UV–vis absorption of Ni@MQDs is similar to that of MQDs, showing a strong absorption peak at 290 nm corresponding to n-π* transition (Manikandan et al., 2019). Apparently, the absorption peak of Ni@MQDs becomes wider, which is attributed to the electron transfer between Ni and MQDs (Chandra et al., 2014). In the PL spectra (Fig. 3b), MQDs exhibit excitation-dependent PL features and the corresponding emissions cover the wavelength range from blue to green (Fig. 3b). Compared to MQDs, there is a marked decay in the PL intensity of Ni@MQDs (Fig. 3c), suggesting the existence of electron interactions between Ni and MQDs in Ni@MQDs (Manikandan et al., 2019; Guo et al., 2015), consistent with the analysis of UV–vis absorption (Fig. 3a).

The catalytic activity of Ni@MQDs is evaluated through the catalytic reduction of Cr (VI) with HCOOH. As seen in Fig. S3, without catalyst, the characteristic absorption peak of Cr (VI) at 350 nm remains almost unchanged even after standing for 40 min (Fig. S3). Upon the addition of Ni@MQDs, the absorption bands of Cr (VI) decrease and disappear within 10.5 min ( Fig. 4a), with the color of the solution changing from yellow to colorless. The generation of Cr (III) can be confirmed by putting sufficient NaOH into the reaction system, showing a green solution of hexahydroxochromate (III) ions indicative of the presence of Cr (III) (Fig. S14). Control experiments are conducted in the presence of bare Ni NPs (Figs. S4 and S5) (Park et al., 2009) and MQDs under the identical experimental conditions (Fig. S6). It is seen that Ni@MQDs shows the highest catalytic activity with the shortest reaction time, while more reaction time is required for other control catalysts. In addition, the reduction of Cr (VI) with HCOOH is carried out under the same experimental conditions using Ni(OH)2 catalyst (Fig. S7). The characteristic absorption peak of Cr (VI) at 350 nm remains unchanged after 30 min, verifying that Ni(OH)2 is almost catalytically inert compared with bare Ni NPs (Fig. S5).

Fig. 4b shows the ln (Ct/C0) kinetic diagrams of different catalysts, in which the increased kinetic rate constant (k = − ln (C t/C 0)/t) (Celebi et al., 2016; Nasrollahzadeh et al., 2018; Sane et al., 2018) follows the order of Ni@MQDs (0.4779 min−1) > Ni NPs (0.0078 min−1) > MQDs (0.0018 min−1), highlighting the positive role of MQDs in accelerating the reaction. Fig. 4c shows the kinetic plots of Cr (VI) reduction over Ni@MQDs at various temperatures ranging from 25 °C to 45 °C. The corresponding UV–vis absorption for the reduction of Cr (VI) over Ni@MQDs at various temperatures is shown in Fig S8. According to the Arrhenius equation (Eq. (2)), the activation energy (E
a ) of Ni@MQDs is calculated to be 18.9 kJ mol−1. The low E
a indicates that Ni@MQDs can effectively reduce the energy barrier for HCOOH to reduce Cr (VI) (Celebi et al., 2016; Lopez and Mangolini, 2014; Lv et al., 2020).
(2) ln k = -E
a / (RT) + lnA

Table 1 shows the comparison of the catalytic performance of Ni@MQDs with other catalysts in the literature. Obviously, Ni@MQDs is superior to that of most reported catalysts, highlighting the critical role of MQDs in significantly promoting the catalytic reaction.

To highlight the effect of HCOOH, we conduct the reduction of Cr (VI) using NaBH4 and ethanol as reducing agents (Figs. S9 and S10). Obviously, NaBH4 maintains the pH of the mixture at 7–8, and the reaction is found to proceed at very slow rates with or without Ni@MQDs (Fig. S9). After adding ethanol (pH 6–7), Cr (VI) can not be reduced without Ni@MQDs (Fig. S10a), while in the presence of Ni@MQDs, the Cr (VI) reduction rate is very slow (Fig. S10b). Therefore, it can be concluded that both NaBH4 and ethanol have little effect on the reduction of Cr (VI) at room temperature.

There are two possible mechanisms for reducing Cr (VI) with HCOOH (Josue et al., 2020; Lv et al., 2020). The chemical reactions are described as follows:
(3) HCOOH → H2 + CO2
(4) Cr2O7 2- + 3H2 + 8H+→ 2Cr3+ + 7H2O
(5) HCOOH → CO + H2O
(6) Cr2O7 2- +3CO + 8H+ → 2Cr3+ + 3CO2 + 4H2O

To prove the dominant mechanism of Cr (VI) reduction process, instead of HCOOH, the gas products of H2 or CO are injected into the reaction system to react with Cr (VI) in the presence of Ni@MQDs. Notably, compared to HCOOH (Fig. 4a), the Cr (VI) concentration with H2 decreases slightly within 70 min (Fig. S11a). This phenomenon can be explained by the fact that the H2 in-situ generated (H*) is more active than that of commercial-available H2 ( Scheme 2) (Abdeta et al., 2021; Celebi et al., 2017). On the other hand, in the presence of CO, the concentration of Cr (VI) decreases slightly, indicating that the CO reduction does not occur in the Ni@MQDs system (Fig. S11b) (Josue et al., 2020).

To further unveil the catalytic mechanism, we conduct the experiments with the co-existence of catalyst and HCOOH (without Cr (VI)) (Fig. S13). The gas products are injected into the NaOH solution containing phenolphthalein as an indicator, and it presents pink color in a basic solution (Fig. S13a). After the injection of the gas products, the pink solution becomes gradually colorless (Fig. S13b), thereby verifying that CO2, rather than CO, is the dominant gas product in the reaction with NaOH to generate neutral salt solution. Through this experiment, it can be proved that HCOOH is decomposed into CO2 and H2 through a dehydrogenation pathway at room temperature. Besides, when the decomposition process takes place on Ni (111) planes, there is a more prominent tendency to transform through dehydrogenation pathway rather than dehydration pathway, due to the much low reaction barrier of dehydration pathway (Lahmar et al., 2017).

The recyclability of the Ni@MQDs is equally important for practical applications. After five successive cycles, the catalyst has a steady conversion rate of more than 91%, indicating the favorable cycle performance of Ni@MQDs (Figs. 4e, f and S12). The slight loss of catalytic activity may be attributed to the waste of catalyst during recovery in the cycle experiment. TEM and XRD analyses of the recycled Ni@MQDs indicate that the morphology and structure of the catalyst are well maintained after five times reused, suggesting that the excellent stability of Ni@MQDs. Fig. 5.

DFT calculations are performed to aid the further understanding of Ni@MQDs in promoting the catalytic reaction of Cr (VI) reduction with HCOOH. It is well established that the effective adsorption of both HCOOH and active hydrogen atoms (Hact) on catalyst surface plays an essential role in efficiently catalyzing the Cr (VI) reduction reaction (Park et al., 2017). As shown in Fig. 6a–c, the HCOOH molecule barely adsorbs on either Ni or MQDs alone, as evidenced by the negligible electron transfer and relatively large adsorption energy (E = –0.68 eV for Ni and E = –0.12 eV for MQDs) (Chu et al., 2020d). In stark contrast, Ni@MQDs shows a much lowered E and a prominent −0.11 |e| is injected into the absorbed HCOOH, indicating the largely strengthened HCOOH stabilization on Ni@MQDs, which favors the C-H cleavage of HCOOH to produce more Hact participating in Cr (VI) reduction reaction (Park et al., 2017). Similarly, for Hact adsorption (Fig. 6d–f), Ni@MQDs exhibits a lower E (−6.19 eV) than Ni (−5.14 eV) and MQDs (−3.79 eV). Therefore, the interaction of Ni with MQDs facilitates the adsorption of both HCOOH and Hact, resulting in the most favorable catalytic activity of Ni@MQDs for Cr (VI) reduction.

We also perform the calculations using the Hubbard-corrected functional by adding U parameter of 6.3 eV for Ni 3d state (Chu et al., 2019c; Guss et al., 2014; Ruizpreciado et al., 2015). It is found that the added parameter U makes the adsorption energies become more negative but the above trend still remains. Also, the Ni particle size effect can be ignored as the calculated adsorption energies between Ni cluster model and Ni slab model are quite same (Fig. S15). Moreover, as catalyst support, MQDs are superior to graphene in boosting the adsorption of HCOOH and Hact (Fig. S16).

For transition metal-based catalysts, the adsorption ability of adsorbates is highly related to the d-band center (εd) of the catalysts (Chu et al., 2021). Hence, we conduct the projected density of states (PDOS) to analyze εd position of Ni atoms in both Ni and Ni@MQDs ( Fig. 7a), which shows that εd of Ni@MQDs moves to a higher energy of −1.423 eV in relation to Ni (−1.937 eV). According to the d-band theory (Stamenkovic et al., 2007), the higher εd can offer more empty antibonding orbit to enhance the interaction between the catalyst and adsorbates. Hence, the excellent catalytic activity of Ni@MQDs originates from the Ni-MQDs interaction that causes an up-shift of εd position for active Ni sites, leading to the enhanced HCOOH/Hact adsorption and reduced reaction energy barrier for Cr (VI) reduction.

Further, the differential charge density shown in Fig. 7b clearly displays the existence of prominent electron interaction between Ni and MQDs across their contact interface (Chu et al., 2020c), which can bring about the improved conductivity of Ni@MQDs. This can be further verified by the more occupied electron state at the Fermi level for Ni@MQDs (Fig. 7c) relative to sole Ni (Fig. 7d) (Li et al., 2020), together with the experimental evidence from electrochemical impedance spectra (Fig. S17), showing a smaller charge-transport resistance of Ni@MQDs than pure Ni (Chu et al., 2019a, 2017; Tian et al., 2019). The accelerated electron transportation ability of Ni@MQDs facilitates the catalytic reaction kinetics, thereby further expediting the catalytic performance for Cr (VI) reduction.

In summary, we have successfully prepared Ni@MQDs by a facile reduction reaction. The TEM investigations clearly revealed that Ni nanoflowers were uniformly coated with ultra-thin MQDs layers. On the other hand, Ni@MQDs had a lower activation energy (E
a = 18.9 kJ mol−1) and a higher kinetic constant (k = 0.4779 min−1), showing the excellent Cr (VI) reduction catalytic performance, which could be ascribed to their large surface areas and more active sites. DFT calculations further revealed that the exceptional catalytic activity of Ni@MQDs originated from the strong Ni-MQDs interaction that caused an up-shift of εd position for active Ni sites, leading to the enhanced HCOOH/Hact adsorption and reduced reaction energy barrier for Cr (VI) reduction. Meanwhile, Ni@MQDs possessed the improved conductivity that favored the catalytic reaction kinetics. These findings imply Ni@MQDs is a promising catalyst for the treatment of Cr (VI) polluted wastewater.

Yali Guo: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yonghua Cheng: Validation, Resources, Data curation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Xingchuan Li: Resources, Investigation, Data curation, Visualization. Qingqing Li: Software, Data curation, Visualization. Xiaotian Li: Validation, Investigation, Resources. Ke Chu: Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Youth Science Foundation of Lanzhou Jiaotong University (2019001), and Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province (21JR1RA247, 20JR10RA241).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2021.127053.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel AgMoOS bimetallic oxysulfide catalyst for highly efficiency catalytic reduction of organic dyes and Chromium (VI
